coredata.dc:title: Enhancing luminescence of AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) by the use of carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Lisha
Ji, Yudong
Li, Weidong
Zhang, Bing
Ma, Qiang
Qin, Bowen
Zhang, Yilei
Yue, Dan
Wang, Zhenling
coredata.dc:description: Lanthanide ions-doped fluoride nanocrystals have excellent application prospect. However, low photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is usually along with lanthanide ions-doped fluoride nanocrystals owing to their low molar absorptivity and narrow absorption band. In order to increase the absorption cross section of Ln3+ in fluoride nanocrystals, a facile synthesis strategy to enhance luminescence of AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) by the use of carbon dots (CDs) has been developed in this work. A series of characterizations including TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS demonstrated that the CDs have been successfully combined with the surface of AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals. The combination of CDs has little influence on the microstructure and phase structure of AF2: Ln3+, and CDs@AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals have uniform and regular shape with cubic phase. CDs are used as a broadband sensitizer and lead to the broadband excitation of Ln3+, resulting in the enhancement around 10-fold of Ln3+ luminescence emission in AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals after CDs combination. The corresponding PLQY increases almost three times, and the luminescence enhancement mechanism by combining CDs is proposed. In addition, the CDs@AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals are used to prepare CDs@AF2: Ln3+/PVA films, which exhibits excellent luminescence properties under the excitation of UV light. This work provides a superior method for enhancing the luminescence properties of lanthanide ions-doped nanocomposites and demonstrates practical potential as promising candidates for light-converting thin films.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide ions
Fluoride nanocrystals
Carbon dots
Luminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals
Synthesis of CDs@AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals
Synthesis of CDs@AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb)/PVA films
Luminescent properties of AF2: Ln3+ (Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals
Luminescent properties of CDs@AF2: Ln3+ (Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) CaF2: xmol%Eu3+ nanocrystals and the local magnified XRD patterns from 27° to 30°, (b) CaF2: Eu3+, CDs@CaF2: 9 mol%Eu3+ and amplified region of peak (111), and (c) Rietveld refinement of CDs@CaF2: 9 mol%Eu3+ nanocrystals.
TEM, HRTEM images and SAED patterns of (a–c) CaF2: Eu3+ and (e–f) CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals. Insets in (a) and (d) are the particle size distribution diagrams of the corresponding samples.

(a) FTIR spectra of CDs, CaF2: Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2: Eu3+, XPS spectra of (b) CaF2: Eu3+ and (c) CDs@CaF2: Eu3+, high-resolution XPS spectra of (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, (f) N 1s for CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals.

(a) The UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of the CDs. (b) PL emission spectra of CDs excited from 340 to 440 nm. (c) PL excitation and emission spectra of CDs-CaF2 without Ln3+. (d) The PL emission spectrum of the CDs-CaF2 (blue line, λex = 240 nm) and the excitation spectrum of CaF2: Eu3+ (red line) revealing the overlap between them. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

PL emission spectra of (a) CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ and (b) CDs@SrF2: Eu3+ under 256 and 392 nm excitation, (c) CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ (red line) and CaF2: Eu3+ (bule line), (d) CDs@SrF2: Eu3+ (red line) and SrF2: Eu3+ (bule line), (e) CDs@CaF2: Tb3+ (green line) and CaF2: Tb3+ (bule line), and (f) CDs@SrF2: Tb3+ (green line) and SrF2: Tb3+ (bule line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of PVA, SrF2: Eu3+/PVA film and CDs@SrF2: Eu3+/PVA film, (b) PL emission spectra of SrF2: Eu3+/PVA and CDs@SrF2: Eu3+/PVA films (Insets show comparative images of the SrF2: Eu3+/PVA film (left column) and CDs@SrF2: Eu3+/PVA film (right column) under irradiation of 254 nm UV lamp).

Energy level diagram for CDs@AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals. Energy transfer (ET) process from CDs to Eu3+ and Tb3+, electron capture (EC) process of CDs, cross-relaxation (CR1 and CR2) mechanisms between two neighboring Tb3+ ions, as well as the emission process from Tb3+, Eu3+, and CDs.

Decay curves of (a) CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ (λex = 256 nm, λem = 590 nm), (b) CDs@SrF2: Eu3+ (λex = 256 nm, λem = 612 nm); (c) CDs@CaF2: Tb3+ (λex = 257 nm, λem = 542 nm), (d) CDs@SrF2: Tb3+ (λex = 257 nm, λem = 545 nm).

head.ce:title: Enhancing luminescence of AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) by the use of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Engineering, Henan International Joint Laboratory of Rare Earth Composite Materials, Henan University of Engineering, Zhengzhou, 451191, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan International Joint Laboratory of Rare Earth Composite Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Material Engineering, Henan International Joint Laboratory of Rare Earth Composite Materials, Henan University of Engineering, Zhengzhou, 451191, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xinxiang Chemical Fiber Co., Ltd, Xinxiang, 453000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xinxiang Chemical Fiber Co., Ltd, Xinxiang, 453000, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Canterbury, New Zealand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Canterbury, New Zealand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lanthanide ions-doped fluoride nanocrystals have excellent application prospect. However, low photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) is usually along with lanthanide ions-doped fluoride nanocrystals owing to their low molar absorptivity and narrow absorption band. In order to increase the absorption cross section of Ln3+ in fluoride nanocrystals, a facile synthesis strategy to enhance luminescence of AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) by the use of carbon dots (CDs) has been developed in this work. A series of characterizations including TEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS demonstrated that the CDs have been successfully combined with the surface of AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals. The combination of CDs has little influence on the microstructure and phase structure of AF2: Ln3+, and CDs@AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals have uniform and regular shape with cubic phase. CDs are used as a broadband sensitizer and lead to the broadband excitation of Ln3+, resulting in the enhancement around 10-fold of Ln3+ luminescence emission in AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals after CDs combination. The corresponding PLQY increases almost three times, and the luminescence enhancement mechanism by combining CDs is proposed. In addition, the CDs@AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals are used to prepare CDs@AF2: Ln3+/PVA films, which exhibits excellent luminescence properties under the excitation of UV light. This work provides a superior method for enhancing the luminescence properties of lanthanide ions-doped nanocomposites and demonstrates practical potential as promising candidates for light-converting thin films.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) are usually considered as a zero-dimensional carbon material which was accidently noticed by Xu et al., in 2004 [1,2]. Considering the ultrafine size, ease of synthesis process and surface modification, light stability, low toxicity, superior water solubility, etc., CDs are acquiring ever greater attention as an innovative material [3–5]. The potential application areas of CDs have been vastly pursued, ranging from optical devices, photochemical catalysis, sensors, bioimaging and so on [6–9]. Recently, considerable focus has been dedicated to the incorporation of CDs into substrate materials to enhance the pristine performance of the material or to yield new properties in some cases. For instance, CDs/CdS–S can be prepared by introducing CDs on CdS and used as a photocatalyst, and its hydrogen production rate in seawater increases 265-fold [10]. The robustness of Mn-CDs assembly in improving tumor hypoxia and strengthening photodynamic therapy (PDT) was investigated by Jia et al. [11]. Carbon-dot-Cu2+ nano-assemblies can realize fluorescence imaging and tumor synergistic therapy [12]. CDs enable ZnO nanorods to be sensitive to visible light and used them to construct solid-state nanostructured solar cells [13].

The application of CDs to enhance the luminescence property of lanthanide ions-doped materials has gained much attention. Lanthanide ions-doped materials are important strategic resources due to the unique 4f electron layer of Ln3+, generally exhibiting narrow emission spectral bands, high color purity, high light-absorbing ability and conversion rate, excellent light stability [14,15]. However, the 4f electron transitions of Ln3+ are usually prevented by the parity selection rule, resulting in lanthanide ions-doped luminescent materials with low photoexcitation efficiency and luminescence intensity. The formation of lanthanide coordination compounds is an important strategy to improve their luminescence efficiency, which is based on sensitizing the luminescence of Ln3+ by exciting organic ligands with high absorption coefficients [16]. Xu et al. developed a novel Ir-based triple excited state complex that is effective in amplifying the near-infrared luminescence of lanthanide ions-doped nanoparticles in contrast to conventional organic antennas [17]. Recently, combining lanthanide and CDs to form Ln-CDs hybrids has also been reported, which possesses excellent luminescence properties and tunable emission modes. Xu et al. prepared NaYF4: Yb,Tm@SiO2/carbon dot nanocomposites, which has dual-mode luminescence properties and exhibits blue green light and blue purple light under different excitation wavelengths [18]. The combination greatly expands the potential versatility of Ln-CDs such as white LED, information encryption, cell imaging, light conversion films, dual-modal imaging, fluorescent probes and so on [19–21]. For instance, CDs combined with NaYF4: Yb, Er could convert near-infrared radiation into red light required for plant growth, thus increase the photosynthetic rate [22]. Changing the ratio of Eu3+ to CDs in KLa(MoO4)2: Eu3+@CDs can realize “warm”, “neutral”, and “cold” white light emission [23]. Li et al. developed PVA/(CQDs)-Eu(III) microrods electrospun NFs film, after touching the film by hand, blue fingerprint pattern will appear on the film under UV light, while fingerprints on the film cannot be seen in daylight, which can be used for fingerprint security [24]. Our group has also reported the related work on CDs enhancing the luminescence properties of lanthanide ions-doped materials such as CDs@AWO4: Yb3+, Er3+ (A = Ca, Sr, Ba), CDs@CePO4: Tb3+, CDs@NaYF4: Yb, Er (Tm) composites and so on [25–27].

Lanthanide ions-doped fluoride nanocrystals deserve critical focus in the optics field mainly on account of their low-energy phonons, wide band gap, excellent biocompatibility, and good thermal stability [28,29]. Among of fluoride materials, AF2 (A = Ca, Sr) is an excellent host regardless of up-conversion or downshifting luminescence [30]. Alkaline-earth ions (Ca2+, Sr2+) can be easily replaced by trivalent lanthanide ions owing to their similar ionic radii. Even if the charge compensation is required, overall ionic charge balance is easily implemented through the formation of defects [31,32]. Nevertheless, AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals usually exhibit low PLQY with regard to surface defects, low molar absorptivity, and narrow absorption range. The luminescence performance of AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals is closely correlated with the morphology and size, but its nucleation and growth processes are still hardly controllable. Meanwhile, the preparation of strongly emissive AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals remain a challenge due to the surface quenching effect [33]. The weak luminescence has restricted its application in bio-detection, bio-imaging, lasers, illumination and optical storage devices [34–36]. Therefore, there is an urgent demand for developing the synthesis strategy of AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals with excellent luminescent properties.

So far, it is not clear if the combination with CDs can improve the luminescent properties of AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) materials. Here, we propose a strategy via combining CDs to AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals to improve the luminescence performance. The results demonstrate that after combination with CDs, the phase structure and morphology of the AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) materials show mainly no change, whereas the luminescence intensities of all materials are appreciably increased. Compared with the samples without CDs, the PL emission intensities increase 14-fold for CDs@CaF2: Eu3+, 10-fold for CDs@SrF2: Eu3+, 8-fold for CDs@CaF2: Tb3+, and 6-fold for CDs@SrF2: Tb3+. This work offers a facile strategy to improve the luminescence properties of lanthanide ions-doped fluoride nanocrystals. We further fabricated CDs@AF2: Ln3+/PVA films and provided a candidate material for light-conversion film, demonstrating the universality of the strategy used to combine with CDs in enhancing the luminescence intensity of lanthanide ions-doped fluoride nanocrystals.

Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (99.99 %), NaF (99 %) and N, N-Dimethylformamide(DMF) were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. Tb(NO3)3·6H2O (99.99 %), calcium acetate and 1,2,4,5-Benzenetetracarboxylic acid were obtained from Shanghai Sarn Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. Strontium acetate was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: CaF2: x%Eu3+ (x = 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 17), CaF2: x%Tb3+ (x = 1,3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, 17), SrF2: x%Eu3+/Tb3+ (x = 5, 7, 9, 13, 15, 17) nanocrystals were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method. A typical procedure for the synthesis of CaF2: 9%Eu3+ nanocrystals was performed as follows: solution 1 was prepared via adding Ca(CH3COOH)2 (2.28 mM) and Eu(NO3)3 (0.228 mM) to 30 mL deionized water and vigorously magnetic stirring for 10 min. Then NaF (5.7 mM) was dissolved in 30 mL distilled water to form solution 2. Afterward, solution 2 was slowly dropwise added to the solution 1. The white turbid solution obtained by additional agitation for 20 min was transferred to a Teflon bottle (100 mL), placed in a stainless-steel autoclave, sealed, and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the white turbid solution was separated by centrifugation after cooling to room temperature, then washed three times with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 80 °C for 24 h to obtain a white powder. CaF2: x%Tb3+, SrF2: x%Eu3+ and SrF2: x%Tb3+ nanocrystals were obtained by the similar procedure.

CDs were synthesized based on the previously described procedures with 1,2,4,5-Benzenetetracarboxylic acid as the carbon source in the presence of dimethylformamide [37]. CDs solution obtained after the hydrothermal reaction was further purified with a dialysis bag (500 Da) to remove DMF and other unreacted precursors to acquire high purity CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs@AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs@AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) were prepared in a process similar to the one used for the preparation of CaF2: Ln3+ (Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals, in exception to adding various volumes of CDs solutions to white turbid solution in the preliminary stage. The remainder of the routine is the same as that above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs@AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb)/PVA films
ce:section.ce:para: 6.0 g of PVA was dissolved in 50 mL distilled water, heated under stirring until the PVA was completely dissolved, then added 0.5 g sodium dodecyl sulfate and 2.5 g glycerin to assist in mold release. 0.1 g of CDs@CaF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals were then added to the 2.0 g PVA solution and stirred until a homogeneous gel-like liquid was formed. CDs@AF2: Ln3+/PVA films were fabricated using a four-sided wet film preparator with a thickness of about 150 μm.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements in order to access the elemental composition of the prepared samples were measured using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha instrument. X-ray diffraction patterns for phase structure of the resulting nanocomposite were noted using a powder X-ray diffractometer from Bruker, Germany, irradiated with Cu Kα (λ = 0.1541 nm) at a scanning rate of 10°/min at 40 kV and 40 mA. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis to determination surface functional groups was performed using a Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrometer in the spectral range of 4000–500 cm−1 by the KBr press method. Morphology of nanocrystals was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100 plus) and (TEM Tecnai T20, FEI). The photoluminescence excitation/emission spectra and lifetime of the samples were measured via the FLS1000 Fluorescence Test System (Edinburgh). The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was assessed by an integrating sphere connected by FLS1000 apparatus.

Representative XRD patterns of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals are given in Fig. 1 a. All peaks of the obtained samples correspond to pure CaF2 phase (PDF#75–0097). The intense and sharp diffraction peaks of the samples are strong, which indicates good crystallinity of the obtained nanocrystals. Furthermore, the main diffraction peak continuously shifted to a lower 2θ angle with increasing Eu3+ doping concentration, which shows an expansion of lattice parameters. These concentration effects have been previously observed in Eu3+/Tb3+-doped CaF2 and attributed to lattice distortion due to the gradual incorporation of interstitial F− [38]. The substitution of Ca2+ ion by a smaller Eu3+ ion with higher positive charge, according to the substitution mechanism, result in structural defects to compensate for the additional charge in order to maintain charge neutrality, especially an interstitial fluorine ions defect (F− with an ionic radius of 136 p.m.) is produced: Ca2+ → Eu3+ + F− [39].

Especially, there are no diffraction peaks of CDs in Fig. 1b. This may be a consequence of the fact as the crystallization peaks of the CaF2 host are too strong and the volume of the added CDs is relatively small. Moreover, the diffraction peaks are not shifted indicating that the CDs have no effect on phase structure of the CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals.

To gain more insight into the changes in the crystal structure, the XRD data of representative CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals were analyzed using the General Structure and Analysis System (GSAS)-Ⅱ program [40], and its refined pattern is shown in Fig. 1c. It is evident that the calculated data are in well agreement with the experimental model. All data consistently satisfy the reflection condition, indicating that the powder is a pure cubic structure with an Fm-3m space group. The unit cell parameter of CaF2 is a = 0.5462 nm, while that of CDs@CaF2: 9%Eu3+ nanocrystals is a = 0.5490 nm, and the cell volume (V) expands slightly from 0.1629 to 0.1655 nm3. When Tb3+ was doped, as shown in Fig. S1, CDs also have shown negligible effect on the phase structure of the CaF2: Tb3+ nanocrystal.

In an attempt to work out the microstructure of CaF2: Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals, TEM, HRTEM and SAED patterns of CaF2: Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals are illustrated in Fig. 2 . CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals (Fig. 2a) exhibit homogeneous and regular shape with the mean size about 22.5 nm (inset of Fig. 2a). It can be seen from Fig. 2b that only the 0.31 nm (111) and 0.19 nm (220) lattice spacings of CaF2: Eu3+ can be indexed. Furthermore, the SAED patterns of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals (Fig. 2c) can be found in the four planes of the cube phase structure of the CaF2 crystals (111), (220), (311) and (400), which are consistent with the XRD results. TEM, HRTEM and XRD pattern of CDs are shown in Fig. S2, the CDs are uniformly dispersed without aggregation. The inset HRTEM image demonstrated that the lattice spacing is 0.33 nm, corresponding to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon. After the combination of CDs into CaF2: Eu3+, CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals (Fig. 2d) retained its morphology analogous to that of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals and the composite nanocrystals particle size was 20 nm (inset of Fig. 2d), revealing that the CDs had no detrimental effect on the morphology of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals. On the one hand, the slightly decreased particle size might be associated with factors affecting grain formation. The addition of CDs promotes the formation of nuclei in solution, resulting in the number of nuclei increases rapidly. And the more the number of crystal nuclei per unit volume, the smaller the grains. On the other hand, several literatures have reported the ability of CDs to exhibit inhibition of nanocrystal agglomeration and growth [41]. The abundant functional groups (-COOH, –OH, etc.) on the surface of CDs are able to form hydrogen bonds with the F− on the surface of the nanocrystals to form relatively stable composite nanocrystals. Nanocrystals might be confined between the CDs, thus effectively preventing nanocrystals from growing during the reaction [42]. It can be seen from the HRTEM analysis (Fig. 2e) that the presence of lattice fringes with spacings of 0.31 nm and 0.33 nm, correctly refer to the (111) plane of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals and the (002) plane of CDs, respectively [43]. From the SAED patterns of CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals (Fig. 2f), the planes (002) of CDs can be explicitly indexed in addition to the planes (111), (220) and (311) of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals. This is further confirmed that the CDs can be combined on the surface of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals.

To demonstrate the CDs combined on the surface of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals, the particles before and after combining CDs were explored by FTIR absorption spectroscopy. FTIR spectra of CaF2: Eu3+ and CaF2: Tb3+ nanocrystals are basically identical. The representative FTIR spectra of CDs, CaF2: Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ are shown in Fig. 3 a. The broadband beyond 3000 cm−1 consists of the O–H stretching vibrations that can be traced back to the remnant water of the crystallites. For CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals, the absorption bands belonging to O–H and N–H stretching vibrations are more intense as a result of the addition of CDs with a large proportion of hydrophilic groups. Compared with CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals, CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals show the peaks at 1595 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1, likely related to the stretching vibrations of the –COO group of CDs. Also, the 1400 cm−1 is the absorption band of C=N [44].

XPS was used to evaluate the elemental composition of CaF2: Eu3+ and CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals for further investigate the combination of CDs with CaF2: Eu3+ (Fig. 3b–f). Comparing Fig. 3b and c, these two samples comprise Eu 3d, F 1s, O 1s, Ca 2p, and C 1s, situated in 1135.5 eV, 685.2 eV, 532.2 eV, 348.2 eV, and 258.2 eV respectively. The presence of N (400.4 eV) (Fig. 3f) in CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals is possibly related to the fact that the CDs were synthesized using DMF as solvent. There are no obvious peaks of N element for the single CaF2: Eu3+ (Fig. S3). XPS plays a crucial role in acquiring composition and bonding information of CDs. C 1s carries three component peaks located at 288.8 eV for the carboxyl group, 286.0 eV for the sp3 hybridized carbon counterpart to C–O or C–N, and 284.8 eV as sp2 carbon responds to C=C bonds (Fig. 3d). The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3e) reveal that oxygen in the samples present in both C=O (531.5 eV) on one side and C–O (533.1 eV) on the other, denoting the presence of oxygen-rich groups, in concordance with the results of the FTIR spectra. In summary, the CDs have been successfully combined on the surface of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals, enabling CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals with an abundant set of functional groups, especially –NH, –OH, and –COOH functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent properties of AF2: Ln3+ (Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S4a reveals the PL emission spectra of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals in various Eu3+ doping concentration under 392 nm wavelength excitation. With the increasing of Eu3+ doping concentration, the PL emission intensities increase, reach the maximum at 9 %, and then decrease, demonstrating that concentration quenching had occurred, which resulted in decreased luminescence intensity. Subsequently, to investigate the optimally doping concentration of Tb3+ in CaF2, the PL emission spectra of CaF2: xmol%Tb3+ (x = 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15 and 17) nanocrystals are illustrated in Fig. S4b. The tendency for the PL emission spectra of CaF2: Tb3+ nanocrystals show its analogous with that of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals, but with an optimal doping concentration at 5 %. In addition, the optimum doping concentration of SrF2: Eu3+ is 7 % (Fig. S4c) and that of SrF2: Tb3+ is 13 % (Fig. S4d). In the succeeding part of the discussion, we selected CaF2: 9%Eu3+, CaF2: 5%Tb3+, SrF2: 7%Eu3+, and SrF2: 13%Tb3+ nanocrystals possessing optimized luminescence properties as typical samples and examined their luminescence properties, composites formed, and luminescence-enhancing properties.

Prior to the preparation of the composite nanocrystals, the fluorescence properties of CDs were previously investigated. We recorded its UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (Fig. 4 ). It is evident that the UV–vis absorption spectrum is characterized by two UV absorption peaks at 245 and 310 nm, being assigned to the π - π* transition from C = C and the n - π* transition from C = O in the CDs [23]. With the excitation of the CDs at 366 nm, there was a maximum emission band observed at 450 nm (Fig. 4a). As the excitation wavelength was increased from 340 nm to 440 nm (Fig. 4b), their broadband emission peaks were red-shifted. In general, the excitation-dependent properties of the CDs also account for the inhomogeneous distribution of the sample particle size and surface state [45].

The PL emission spectrum recorded for CDs showed strong emission in the 400–600 nm range. We have also explored the excitation and emission spectra of CDs combined with hosts without any lanthanide ion, as depicted in Fig. 4c. Interestingly, it can be noticed that the position of the emission peaks is blue-shifted to 325–500 nm range, overlapping with the excitation peaks of Eu3+ and Tb3+. The blue-shift phenomenon here could be mainly attributed to the introduction of F− in CDs, which can form hydrogen bonds with the functional groups (such as –OH, –COOH) on the surface of CDs. The introduction of electron withdrawing functional groups (like -F-) and steric hindrance effects would damage the conjugation effect in the carbon nucleus, leading to a blue-shift in the fluorescence of CDs [46]. In order to verify the energy transfer, we measured the excitation spectrum of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals and the emission spectrum of CDs-CaF2 (Fig. 4d). It can be seen that the sharp excitation peaks arising from the f-f transitions of the Eu3+ ion are located at 363 nm (7F0-5D4), 382 nm (7F0-5G2), 394 nm (7F0-5L6), 415 nm (7F0-5D3), and 464 nm (7F0-5D2) [47]. Whereas the distinctive excitation peaks of Tb3+ ions are situated at 350 nm (7F6-5D2), 367 nm (7F6-5G6) as well as 378 nm (7F6-5D3) (Fig. S5), which shows the possibility of the energy transfer between Eu3+ or Tb3+ and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent properties of CDs@AF2: Ln3+ (Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: The PL emission spectra of CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ and CDs@SrF2: Eu3+ under 256 and 392 nm excitation are presented in Fig. 5 a and b. Both of them exhibit the strongest emission peaks under 256 nm. The emission peaks at 578, 590, 612, 649, and 695 nm, can be attributed to 5D0→7F0, 5D0→7F1, 5D0→7F2, 5D0→7F3, and 5D0→7F4 transitions of Eu3+, respectively. Eu3+ is commonly used to investigate the symmetry of the host local crystalline field. The magnetic dipole (5D0-7F1) transition is recognized as insensitive due to its insensitivity to changes in the lattice symmetry [48]. On the contrary, the 5D0→7F2 electric dipole transition is forbidden in a center-symmetric environment. The relative intensity of the 5D0→7F2 and 5D0→7F1 transitions is described as the asymmetry ratio indicating the symmetry of the crystallographic sites at which Eu3+ ion is placed. The lower the asymmetry ratio, the better the local symmetry of the host. Eu3+ may be replacing Ca2+ in CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals, occupying the inversion symmetry positions. The 5D0-7F2 transition is forbidden owing to the high symmetry position of Eu3+, thus bringing about dominant orange emission (Fig. 5a). Moreover, as shown in Fig. 5b, Eu3+ has occupied two different environments in CDs@SrF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals. Under the excitation of 256 nm UV light, the emission intensity produced by surface Eu3+ is stronger than that of internal Eu3+, demonstrating higher asymmetry ratio of Eu3+ on or near the surface of the nanocrystals. At 392 nm UV light excitation, the internal Eu3+ emission is the dominant emission. Consequently, the internal Eu3+ has a lower asymmetry ratio and occupies the symmetry positions.

The CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals, significantly improve the luminescence performance compared to that of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals. The PL emission spectra of a series of CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals which contained diverse amounts of CDs were examined in detail, as illustrated in Fig. S6, and the volumes of CDs were 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6 and 8 mL. Interestingly, the intensity of the emission peaks is remarkably enhanced by increasing the volume of CDs to 6 mL. When the volume of CDs continues to increase, the intensity of the emission peaks gradually decreases. The higher adding content of CDs above 6 mL probably leads to the increase of the quenching centers, which might affect the emission intensity of CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals to some extent. The PL emission intensity of CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals is about 14 times higher than that of CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals under 256 nm excitation (Fig. 5c). Correspondingly, the PLQY is also improved, from 5.41 % for CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals to 18.01 % for CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals. And similarly, the enhancement of CDs@SrF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals relative to without CDs is 10-fold (Fig. 5d). The PLQY increases from 4.26 % for SrF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals to 15.12 % for CDs@SrF2: Eu3+ nanocrystals. We have also performed the PL emission spectra of AF2: Eu3+ before and after combining CDs under 392 nm UV light excitation, and the emission intensity of CaF2: Eu3+ is slightly stronger than that of CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ (Fig. S7). It should be emphasized that the PL emission intensity of Eu3+ in the CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ at 256 nm excitation is much stronger than that at 392 nm excitation. Therefore, we choose the excitation conditions at 256 nm to excite CDs@AF2: Eu3+ composite nanocrystals synthesized in this study.

In addition, the PL emission spectra of CDs@CaF2: Tb3+ and CDs@SrF2: Tb3+ are presented in Fig. 5e and f. The distinguished peaks located at 489, 542, 585 and 620 nm in PL emission spectra are correlated with the Tb3+ ion representative 5D4-7F6, 5, 4, 3 transitions. CDs still exhibit excellently enhanced fluorescence in both CaF2: Tb3+ and SrF2: Tb3+. The PL emission intensity of CDs@CaF2: Tb3+ nanocrystals is roughly 8 times that of CaF2: Tb3+ (Fig. 5e), and the PLQY increases from 3.57 % for CaF2: Tb3+ nanocrystals to 14.18 % for CDs@CaF2: Tb3+ nanocrystals. The PL emission intensity of CDs@SrF2: Tb3+ is enhanced by a factor of 6 relative to without CDs (Fig. 5f). The PLQY is improved from 4.12 % for SrF2: Tb3+ nanocrystals to 17.84 % for CDs@SrF2: Tb3+ nanocrystals. The enhancement of Ln3+ emission in CDs@AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals is caused by the energy transfer from CDs to Ln3+, as verified from the PL excitation spectra of the nanocrystals. After the combination of AF2: Ln3+ with CDs, as shown in Fig. S8, CD@AF2: Ln3+ shows a strong broadband absorption in the range of 250–350 nm compared with AF2: Ln3+, which broadened the absorption cross-section of Ln3+, and this is an important reason for the strong fluorescence of CDs@AF2: Ln3+. Therefore, CDs could be used as a new class of broadband sensitizer to enhance the PL emission intensity of Ln3+.

We further selected CDs@SrF2: Eu3+ to be mixed with PVA for the preparation of CD@SrF2: Eu3+/PVA films. As can be seen from Fig. 6 a, CDs@SrF2: Eu3+/PVA film has a strong absorption in the wavelength region of 210∼400 nm, while SrF2: Eu3+/PVA film has a relatively weak absorption band. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 6b, under 254 nm UV light excitation, the SrF2: Eu3+/PVA film exhibits weak red light, whereas the CDs@SrF2: Eu3+/PVA film emits bright red light, and the photographs of these films under irradiation of 254 nm UV lamp are given in Fig. 6b (insets).

Based on the energy-level diagram of CDs, Eu3+ and Tb3+, Fig. 7 illustrates the possible energy transfer routes. The electrons in the π energy level of C in the CDs are excited at a certain energy, and transfer to the π* energy level of C, then occur an electronic relaxation process from C π* - N π* and N π* - O π* to the O π* (LUMO) energy level [49]. The electrons in O π* energy level can also be transferred to the 5D0 or higher energy levels of Eu3+, which can be non-radiatively relaxed to the 5D0 energy level through multiphoton relaxation. At the same time, CDs can also capture electrons in the 5D0-J energy level and then transfer them by Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process to the 5D0 energy level [50]. Both of them contribute to the increase in the number of electrons in the 5D0 energy level and the enhancement of 5D0→7F1, 2, 3, 4 transitions. Here, the lifetime of CaF2: Eu3+ at 590 nm increases from 8.1 m s to 9.6 m s after the combination of CDs, and the lifetime of SrF2: Eu3+ at 612 nm improves from 3.4 m s to 4.5 m s after CDs combination. (Fig. 8 a and b).

For CDs@CaF2: Tb3+ and CDs@SrF2: Tb3+ nanocrystals, the electrons in O π* energy level can also be transferred to the 5D4 or 5D3 energy level of Tb3+. Following this, the populated 5D3 excited states ions may nonradiative transition to 5D4 or 7F0 states via cross-relaxation energy transfers (CRET), which results in an increased amount of electrons in the 5D4 energy level and enhanced emission of 5D4-7F6, 5, 4, 3. As illustrated in Fig. 8c and d, the reduced emission lifetimes at 542 nm and 545 nm might be the result of an increase in probability of a quench center encounter and defects impacting the nonradiative rate of transition [39,51]. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. S9, we examined lifetime of CDs before and after combining to AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals. The lifetime of CDs is about 4.9 ns, and the average lifetimes of CDs in CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ and CDs@SrF2: Tb3+ nanocrystals are about 4.2 and 4.3 ns respectively, which is lower than that of pure CDs, indicating the existence of energy transfer between CDs and AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals. For evaluating the energy transfer efficiency, using the equation η = 1-(τ/τ0), where τ is the lifetime of the CDs in CDs@AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals and τ0 is the lifetime of the CDs. The calculated energy transfer efficiency is about 14.2 % for CDs@CaF2: Eu3+ and 12.2 % for CDs@SrF2: Tb3+, respectively.

In summary, CDs@AF2: Ln3+ (A = Ca, Sr; Ln = Eu, Tb) nanocrystals were successfully fabricated via combining CDs with AF2: Ln3+ in a one-step hydrothermal method. Utilizing a variety of measurement techniques, it was established that CDs have been successfully combined with AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals, and the combination of CDs has negligible influence towards the microstructure of AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals. However, a notable enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of CDs@AF2: Ln3+ nanocrystals is present compared to AF2: Ln3+ without CDs. This study suggests a promising and simple method for the enhancement of CaF2 and SrF2 nanocrystals doped with lanthanide ions, and other lanthanide ions-doped luminescent materials as well. A prospecting candidate for light-converting thin films is also proposed which will contribute to the further development of nanomaterials applications.

Lisha Zhang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yudong Ji: Methodology, Formal analysis. Weidong Li: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Bing Zhang: Resources, Methodology. Qiang Ma: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Bowen Qin: Validation, Software, Resources. Yilei Zhang: Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Dan Yue: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhenling Wang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
